steps.title: Identify the complex items in the list
steps.content: The blank falls within a description of the app that contains two related parts: “Chickasaw Basic, in 2009” and “an online television network, Chickasaw TV, in 2010.” Notice that each part already has an internal comma—one separates the name from the date, the other separates the descriptor from the alternative name.

steps.title: Apply the semicolon-in-series rule
steps.content:
When a list contains items that each have internal commas, you separate the items with semicolons, not commas. A semicolon after “in 2009” marks where the first description ends and the second begins.

Choice C places the semicolon correctly: “Chickasaw Basic, in 2009; an online television network, Chickasaw TV, in 2010.” Choices A and B place semicolons in wrong positions (after “Basic” alone, which leaves a comma before an incomplete element), and choice D uses only commas throughout, muddying what is actually being listed.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
